questions.q: What is the main difference between heap storage and index-organized storage?
options: Heap storage is faster; index-organized storage is slower
Heap tables store rows in arbitrary order; index-organized tables physically sort and store rows by primary key
Index-organized tables cannot have secondary indexes
Heap storage is only available in NoSQL databases
questions.explanation: Heap storage stores rows in insertion order; lookups require index traversal then row fetches. Index-organized tables (like Oracle IOT or MySQL InnoDB primary key) store rows sorted by primary key, eliminating the extra fetch step for PK queries.

questions.q: What is a database page and why does its size matter?
options: A page is a single row in a table
A page is a fixed-size unit of disk I/O (typically 4-16 KB) containing multiple rows; size affects how many rows fit per disk read and overall I/O efficiency
A page is a backup copy of the database
Page size is irrelevant to database performance
questions.explanation: Pages are the atomic unit of disk I/O. Larger pages mean more rows per read but higher memory cost; smaller pages reduce memory but increase I/O operations. 8 KB is common. Optimal size depends on workload and hardware.

questions.q: What is the buffer pool and why is it crucial for performance?
options: A queue of pending queries waiting to execute
An in-memory cache of frequently accessed disk pages that reduces costly disk I/O
A temporary storage for network messages
A tool for managing user connections
questions.explanation: The buffer pool caches disk pages in RAM. Hitting the buffer pool (page hit) is microseconds; reading from disk is milliseconds. Larger buffer pools reduce I/O. Most databases let you tune buffer pool size as a critical knob.

questions.q: What is WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) and why is it essential?
options: A technique for distributing writes across multiple disks
A method of writing all changes to a log before updating the actual data, enabling recovery from crashes
A compression algorithm for reducing data size
A way to encrypt database files
questions.explanation: WAL ensures durability: before modifying a data page, the change is written to a log on stable storage. On crash, the log is replayed to recover uncommitted changes. Without WAL, power loss could corrupt data.

questions.q: What is write amplification and why is it a concern?
options: Increasing the font size of error messages
A phenomenon where writing X bytes of logical data requires writing Y > X bytes to physical storage, wasting resources
Distributing writes to multiple servers
The process of replicating data for backup
questions.explanation: Write amplification occurs in LSM trees and SSDs: updating one row may rewrite entire SSTables or blocks. If you write 1 KB logically but 10 KB physically, amplification is 10x. This reduces write throughput and lifespan of SSDs.

questions.q: How do B+ tree storage engines optimize for range queries?
options: They only support exact match queries
They maintain sorted order and leaf nodes linked as a doubly-linked list, enabling fast sequential range scans
They use hash tables instead of trees
They compress data before storing it
questions.explanation: B+ trees keep leaves sorted and linked; a range query finds the start leaf via tree traversal, then follows links sequentially. This is far faster than random lookups for each row. LSM trees lack this optimization.

questions.q: What does a storage engine's page layout determine?
options: The color scheme of the user interface
How rows, free space, and metadata are physically arranged within a disk page, affecting insertion, deletion, and scan efficiency
The network bandwidth used by the database
The encryption algorithm applied to data
questions.explanation: Page layout is critical: it determines space utilization, how quickly rows can be located within a page, and fragmentation on deletes. A slotted page design uses a directory at the end; a simple layout packs rows sequentially.

questions.q: Why do some storage engines use mmap (memory-mapped I/O) and others use explicit read/write?
options: mmap is always better than explicit I/O
mmap lets the OS handle paging automatically but may cause unexpected performance; explicit I/O gives more control but requires more code
mmap cannot be used on large databases
Explicit read/write is obsolete technology
questions.explanation: mmap delegates paging to the OS, simplifying code but risking memory pressure and page faults. Explicit I/O (read/write syscalls) lets the engine control the buffer pool and prioritize access patterns. Trade-off: simplicity vs control.

questions.q: What is row versioning in a storage engine?
options: Keeping a backup copy of each row in a separate file
Maintaining multiple versions of rows to support MVCC, allowing old transactions to read consistent snapshots while new writes create new versions
Numbering rows sequentially for sorting
Storing row modification timestamps in a separate table
questions.explanation: Row versioning enables MVCC: each change creates a new version with a transaction ID, while old versions remain readable. Garbage collection removes versions no longer needed by active transactions. This avoids blocking readers.

questions.q: How do checkpoint operations affect database recovery?
options: They prevent any data from being written to disk
They flush dirty pages to disk at regular intervals; on recovery, only the log after the last checkpoint is replayed, speeding up recovery
They create a snapshot for point-in-time restore
They have no impact on recovery speed
questions.explanation: Checkpoints persist the buffer pool to disk, marking a known-clean state. On crash recovery, the system replays only the log after the checkpoint, reducing recovery time significantly. Without checkpoints, the entire log must be replayed.
